speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 6, survivor David Klebanow and wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Klebanow, would you please introduce your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is my-- you know-- you know already, Sophie is my third wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, name, first name was [? Lebet. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Gavendo it is. I [INAUDIBLE] know her-- I-- by name. She is-- was born in [INAUDIBLE]. Gavendo was a famous already at that time warrior.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, your father. When did you get married, Sophie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, so when you-- you were married before.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, married this guy over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to this guy, this guy in 1970. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what has life been like being married to Dr. David Klebanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it hasn't been terrible, but he-- I'm sure during the war and everything that went with it has made things difficult for him, and he is not the easiest man in the world. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you're very lucky to have such a lovely wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let me-- let me ask you. You have grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you tell your grandchildren today? What words of wisdom could you share with them for their future?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very hard to say what I can say, you know? It is-- it is-- don't forget that their mother is a Catholic and the children were brought up in Catholic faith. And they are coming soon. We are very good to them. And they will-- I will see them more often here, but-- but-- I-- her mother had no right, no right to be early-- very early to decide who has to be Jew and who has to be Catholic. She decided on her own, and I am-- I am angry at her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other son? Does he have children?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you going-- what would you like me-- what would you like to tell your children, your own sons about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My own-- my own sons know my life very well. [INAUDIBLE] understand it-- the older son is now 46, and the younger son is now 41. They know my life all through and through, and we have a very good rapport. We have very, good-- good relations. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you, is it possible for something like the war, like Hitler, to happen again in your children's lifetime? Do you see it as a possibility?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe that it can happen in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: It can happen again in Germany. It can happen in Russia. In Russia, I expect it very much and still expect it may happen. It can happen in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the oldest countries in Poland. Poland is a very anti-- up to now, very anti-Semitic, very anti-Semitic. Although they have 4,000 elderly Jews, still very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to end off on a positive note, so I want to ask you for a prayer for all-- for all the babies, in honor of all the babies that you've brought into this world. What would you like to wish for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I can wish them what-- happiness, a quiet, quiet life. I know there is a Chinese curse, you know, you should have an interesting life. I had this interesting life for 40, or over 40 years. I spend here now, 41 years, and it was a very quiet, very fine life. That is a life I can wish them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you both today on behalf of the Shoah Foundation for participating in a very, very wonderful interview. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is in the picture, Dr. Klebanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, Anna Klebanow, born Schlingman. My sister, Liza, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] last name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: It is another boy. This is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] in the corner, the small picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't touch. Don't touch. Don't touch it, Dr. Klebanow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. All right, it is my sister's son.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 19-- the big picture was taken 19-- approximately '25, 1925.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before departure of my-- departure of my sister to Israel, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my first wife, Sacha Derachynski.

speaker: interviewee
text: She taken it approximately in 1938. She's a young doctor, graduated from a medical school in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she die? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1945, approximately two months after liberation from concentration camp of TB of the lungs.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture-- that is the big picture, I am here with my second wife, Gala.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Dr. David Klebanow and Galena Klebanow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken a few days after her first delivery of my older son at the University Hospital of--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, me with my sons, approximately 1985. And then, my older son with his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he in the red shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Galina, Galina is his second daughter, what are you talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is the first baby. You think it's second baby?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, OK. And who's the man--

speaker: interviewee
text: Why you, why you, why you want to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you know it's the second baby?

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and Dr. Klebanow, who's in the white shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the white shirt-- white shirt is my son, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in-- Sophie, I will say, you know, it is taken already in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that picture was taken in Los Angeles.
